- 2026-04-20T01:48:40Z | `checkpoint_rejected` [enrichment] | Enrichment checkpoint rejected; Terry is rerunning enrichment. | notes="# The Policy Gradient Theorem\n\n## Setting\n\nLet $S$ and $A$ be finite sets of **states** and **actions**, with $N = |S||A|$.\nWrite $\\mathcal{SA} = S \\times A$ for the set of state-action pairs.\n\n**Transition kernel.** Let $P(s' \\mid s, a)$ be the probability of transitioning to state\n$s'$ from pair $(s,a)$. This is fixed and does not depend on any parameter.\n\n**Policy.** Let $\\theta \\in \\mathbb{R}^p$ be a parameter vector. A policy is a family of\nconditional distributions $\\pi_\\theta(a \\mid s)$, the probability of choosing action $a$ in\nstate $s$ under parameter $\\theta$. Assume $\\theta \\mapsto \\pi_\\theta(a \\mid s)$ is $C^1$\nfor every $(s, a)$.\n\n**State-action transition matrix.** Define the $N \\times N$ matrix $\\Pi(\\theta)$ by\n\n$$\\Pi(\\theta)\\bigl[(s,a),\\,(s',a')\\bigr] \\;=\\; P(s' \\mid s, a)\\cdot\\pi_\\theta(a' \\mid s').$$\n\nOne multiplication by $\\Pi(\\theta)$ represents one full environment-agent cycle: the\nenvironment moves from $(s,a)$ to $s'$ via $P$, then the agent selects $a'$ via\n$\\pi_\\theta$. Because $\\pi_\\theta$ is $C^1$ in $\\theta$, so is every entry of $\\Pi(\\theta)$,\nand the entrywise derivative is\n\n$$\\frac{\\partial \\Pi}{\\partial \\theta_i}(\\theta)\\bigl[(s,a),(s',a')\\bigr]\n  = P(s' \\mid s,a)\\cdot\\frac{\\partial \\pi_\\theta}{\\partial \\theta_i}(a' \\mid s').$$\n\nWrite $\\Pi'(\\theta) \\in \\mathbb{R}^{p \\times N \\times N}$ for the full derivative tensor, and\n$\\partial_i \\Pi(\\theta)$ for its $i$-th slice.\n\n**Reward vector.** Let $r \\in \\mathbb{R}^N$ be a fixed (parameter-independent) column vector\nwith $r[(s,a)]$ the immediate reward for taking action $a$ in state $s$.\n\n**Initial distribution.** Let $p_0 \\in \\mathbb{R}^N$ be a fixed (parameter-independent) row\nvector, the initial distribution over state-action pairs.\n\n---\n\n## Assumptions\n\n**A1 (Invertibility / Spectral radius).** For all $\\theta$ in an open set\n$\\Theta \\subseteq \\mathbb{R}^p$,\n\n$$\\rho\\bigl(\\Pi(\\theta)\\bigr) < 1,$$\n\nwhere $\\rho$ denotes the spectral radius. Equivalently, the matrix $I - \\Pi(\\theta)$ is\ninvertible, or equivalently, $\\Pi(\\theta)^k \\to 0$ as $k \\to \\infty$.\n\n> **Concrete sufficient conditions.**\n> - *Discounted MDP*: replace $\\Pi(\\theta)$ by $\\gamma\\Pi(\\theta)$ with $\\gamma \\in [0,1)$,\n>   so $\\rho(\\gamma\\Pi) \\leq \\gamma < 1$.\n> - *Episodic MDP*: the underlying Markov chain terminates with probability 1, making the\n>   row sums of $\\Pi(\\theta)$ strictly less than 1 from every non-terminal state, which\n>   forces $\\rho(\\Pi(\\theta)) < 1$.\n\n**A2 (Bounded rewards).** $r \\in \\mathbb{R}^N$ (automatic in finite state-action spaces).\n\n**A3 ($C^1$ policy).** The map $\\theta \\mapsto \\pi_\\theta(a \\mid s)$ is continuously\ndifferentiable on $\\Theta$ for every $(s, a)$, so $\\theta \\mapsto \\Pi(\\theta)$ is $C^1$.\n\n---\n\n## Definitions\n\nUnder A1\u2013A3 the following objects are well-defined and $C^1$ on $\\Theta$.\n\n**State-action value vector.**\n\n$$q(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\sum_{k=0}^{\\infty} \\Pi(\\theta)^k\\, r \\;=\\; \\bigl(I - \\Pi(\\theta)\\bigr)^{-1} r.$$\n\nThe entry $q(\\theta)[(s,a)]$ is the expected total (discounted/episodic) reward starting\nfrom pair $(s,a)$; this is the $Q$-function.\n\n**Visitation frequency vector.**\n\n$$d(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\sum_{k=0}^{\\infty} p_0\\,\\Pi(\\theta)^k \\;=\\; p_0\\bigl(I-\\Pi(\\theta)\\bigr)^{-1}.$$\n\nThe entry $d(\\theta)[(s,a)]$ is the expected number of times pair $(s,a)$ is visited\nover the trajectory.\n\n*Smoothness.* Both $q(\\theta)$ and $d(\\theta)$ are $C^1$ because they are the composition\nof the $C^1$ map $\\theta \\mapsto I - \\Pi(\\theta)$ with matrix inversion, which is a smooth\nmap on the open set of invertible matrices.\n\n**Total reward.**\n\n$$J(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\sum_{k=0}^{\\infty} p_0\\,\\Pi(\\theta)^k\\, r \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\cdot r\n  \\;=\\; p_0 \\cdot q(\\theta).$$\n\n---\n\n## Fixed-Point Identities\n\nShifting the summation index in the series definitions yields the identities\n\n$$d(\\theta) \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\,\\Pi(\\theta) + p_0, \\qquad\n  q(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\Pi(\\theta)\\,q(\\theta) + r,$$\n\nor equivalently\n\n$$p_0 \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\bigl(I - \\Pi(\\theta)\\bigr), \\qquad\n  r \\;=\\; \\bigl(I-\\Pi(\\theta)\\bigr)q(\\theta).$$\n\nUnder A1, $(I - \\Pi(\\theta))$ is invertible, so these equations have the **unique**\nsolutions $d(\\theta) = p_0(I-\\Pi(\\theta))^{-1}$ and $q(\\theta) = (I-\\Pi(\\theta))^{-1}r$.\nUniqueness is essential: it ensures that when we differentiate the fixed-point identities\nbelow, the resulting equations govern the derivatives of our specific $d(\\theta)$ and\n$q(\\theta)$, not those of some other solution.\n\n---\n\n## Theorem (Policy Gradient)\n\nUnder assumptions A1\u2013A3,\n\n$$\\frac{\\partial J}{\\partial \\theta_i}(\\theta)\n  \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\;\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)\\;q(\\theta)\n  \\qquad \\text{for each } i = 1,\\dots,p.$$\n\n---\n\n## Proof\n\n**Step 1: Differentiate the fixed-point identities.**\n\nDifferentiate $p_0 = d(\\theta)(I - \\Pi(\\theta))$ with respect to $\\theta_i$. Since $p_0$\nis independent of $\\theta$:\n\n$$0 \\;=\\; \\partial_i d(\\theta)\\cdot(I-\\Pi(\\theta)) \\;-\\; d(\\theta)\\cdot\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta),$$\n\n$$\\partial_i d(\\theta)\\cdot(I - \\Pi(\\theta)) \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\;\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta). \\tag{1}$$\n\nDifferentiate $r = (I-\\Pi(\\theta))q(\\theta)$ with respect to $\\theta_i$. Since $r$ is\nindependent of $\\theta$:\n\n$$0 \\;=\\; -\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)\\cdot q(\\theta) \\;+\\; (I-\\Pi(\\theta))\\cdot\\partial_i q(\\theta),$$\n\n$$(I-\\Pi(\\theta))\\;\\partial_i q(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)\\;q(\\theta). \\tag{2}$$\n\n**Step 2: Differentiate total reward and substitute.**\n\nUsing $J(\\theta) = d(\\theta)\\cdot r$:\n\n$$\\partial_i J(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\partial_i d(\\theta)\\cdot r.$$\n\nSubstitute $r = (I - \\Pi(\\theta))q(\\theta)$:\n\n$$\\partial_i J(\\theta) \\;=\\; \\partial_i d(\\theta)\\cdot(I-\\Pi(\\theta))\\;q(\\theta).$$\n\nApply (1):\n\n$$\\partial_i J(\\theta) \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\;\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)\\;q(\\theta). \\qquad \\square$$\n\n*Alternatively*, using $J(\\theta) = p_0 \\cdot q(\\theta)$:\n\n$$\\partial_i J(\\theta) \\;=\\; p_0 \\cdot \\partial_i q(\\theta)\n  \\;=\\; d(\\theta)(I-\\Pi(\\theta))\\;\\partial_i q(\\theta)\n  \\;=\\; d(\\theta)\\;\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)\\;q(\\theta),$$\n\nwhere the last step applies (2). Both routes give the same result. $\\square$\n\n---\n\n## Connection to the Classical Statement\n\nThe classical policy gradient theorem (Sutton et al., 1999) states\n\n$$\\nabla_\\theta J(\\theta)\n  \\;\\propto\\; \\sum_{s} d^\\pi(s)\\sum_{a} \\nabla_\\theta \\pi_\\theta(a\\mid s)\\;Q^\\pi(s,a).$$\n\nThis is exactly the formula above in expanded form. Using the factored form of\n$\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)$:\n\n$$\\partial_i J(\\theta)\n  = \\sum_{(s,a)}\\sum_{(s',a')} d(\\theta)[(s,a)]\\;P(s'\\mid s,a)\\;\n    \\frac{\\partial\\pi_\\theta}{\\partial\\theta_i}(a'\\mid s')\\;q(\\theta)[(s',a')].$$\n\nSumming over $(s,a)$ first and using the definition of $d$ collapses this to a sum over\n$(s',a')$ of $\\frac{\\partial\\pi_\\theta}{\\partial\\theta_i}(a'\\mid s')$ weighted by the\n$Q$-value $q(\\theta)[(s',a')]$ and the visitation frequency, recovering the classical form.\n\n---\n\n## Summary of Assumptions\n\n| Label | Assumption | Role |\n|-------|-----------|------|\n| A1 | $\\rho(\\Pi(\\theta)) < 1$ for all $\\theta \\in \\Theta$ | Convergence of series; invertibility of $I-\\Pi$; uniqueness and smoothness of $d, q$ |\n| A2 | $r \\in \\mathbb{R}^N$ bounded | Finiteness of $q(\\theta)$ |\n| A3 | $\\theta \\mapsto \\Pi(\\theta)$ is $C^1$ | $\\partial_i\\Pi(\\theta)$ exists; product rule applies |"

- 2026-04-22T00:20:44Z | `checkpoint_rejected` [plan] | Plan checkpoint rejected; Terry is rerunning the plan stage. | notes="Your Lean statement is only the **algebraic core after differentiating**. It assumes the hard parts:\n\n```lean\nhq_fix  : r = (1 - \u03a0) q\nhd_diff : d' (1 - \u03a0) = d \u03a0'\n```\n\nThose are not self-contained. They bypass the inverse definition of `q` and `d`, and they bypass the derivative of the inverse/resolvent. If the theorem is meant to state the actual policy-gradient identity, then the inverse must appear in the definitions of `q`, `d`, and `J`.\n\nA better self-contained statement is this.\n\n```lean\nimport Mathlib.Data.Matrix.Mul\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse\nimport Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Basic\n\nopen Matrix\n\nnoncomputable section\n\n/--\nSelf-contained coordinate form of the policy-gradient identity.\n\n`Idx` indexes the parameter coordinates.\n`SA` is the finite type of state-action pairs.\n\n`\u03a0 \u03b8` is the state-action transition matrix at parameter vector `\u03b8`.\n\nFor a fixed coordinate `i : Idx`, the expression\n\n    fun x : \u211d => \u03a0 (Function.update \u03b8 i x)\n\nis the one-dimensional slice obtained by varying only the `i`-th coordinate\nof `\u03b8`.\n\n`\u03a0_i'` is the derivative of this coordinate slice at `\u03b8 i`, i.e. the formal\nversion of `\u2202\u1d62 \u03a0(\u03b8)`.\n\nThe theorem defines\n\n    q(\u03b8) = (I - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9 r\n    d(\u03b8) = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9\n    J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r\n\ninside the statement, and concludes\n\n    \u2202\u1d62 J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 (\u03a0_i' q(\u03b8)).\n-/\ntheorem policyGradient_selfContained_coordinate\n    {Idx SA : Type*}\n    [Fintype Idx] [DecidableEq Idx]\n    [Fintype SA] [DecidableEq SA]\n    (\u03a0 : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (p\u2080 r : SA \u2192 \u211d)\n    (\u03b8 : Idx \u2192 \u211d)\n    (i : Idx)\n    (\u03a0_i' : Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n\n    /--\n    `\u03a0_i'` is the derivative of the transition matrix with respect to\n    coordinate `i`, evaluated at `\u03b8`.\n\n    This is the Lean version of\n\n        \u03a0_i' = \u2202\u1d62 \u03a0(\u03b8).\n    -/\n    (h\u03a0_i' :\n      HasDerivAt\n        (fun x : \u211d => \u03a0 (Function.update \u03b8 i x))\n        \u03a0_i'\n        (\u03b8 i))\n\n    /--\n    Resolvent/invertibility hypothesis.\n\n    This corresponds to the mathematical assumption that\n\n        I - \u03a0(\u03b8\u2080)\n\n    is invertible for every parameter value under consideration.\n\n    This assumption is essential. In Mathlib, matrix inverse notation `A\u207b\u00b9`\n    is a total operation, so it exists syntactically even for singular matrices;\n    the nonsingularity hypothesis is what makes it the genuine mathematical\n    inverse.\n    -/\n    (hInv :\n      \u2200 \u03b8\u2080 : Idx \u2192 \u211d, Matrix.det (1 - \u03a0 \u03b8\u2080) \u2260 0) :\n\n    /--\n    Define the value vector at `\u03b8`:\n\n        q(\u03b8) = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r.\n    -/\n    let q : SA \u2192 \u211d :=\n      Matrix.mulVec ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9) r\n\n    /--\n    Define the visitation-frequency vector at `\u03b8`:\n\n        d(\u03b8) = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9.\n    -/\n    let d : SA \u2192 \u211d :=\n      Matrix.vecMul p\u2080 ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9)\n\n    /--\n    Define the total return function:\n\n        J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r\n             = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r.\n    -/\n    let J : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 \u211d :=\n      fun \u03b8\u2080 =>\n        dotProduct\n          (Matrix.vecMul p\u2080 ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8\u2080)\u207b\u00b9))\n          r\n\n    /--\n    Policy-gradient identity for coordinate `i`:\n\n        \u2202\u1d62 J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 (\u03a0_i' q(\u03b8)).\n    -/\n    HasDerivAt\n      (fun x : \u211d => J (Function.update \u03b8 i x))\n      (dotProduct d (Matrix.mulVec \u03a0_i' q))\n      (\u03b8 i) := by\n  sorry\n```\n\nThe same statement can be written without local `let`s, making the inverse completely visible in the final line:\n\n```lean\ntheorem policyGradient_selfContained_coordinate_expanded\n    {Idx SA : Type*}\n    [Fintype Idx] [DecidableEq Idx]\n    [Fintype SA] [DecidableEq SA]\n    (\u03a0 : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (p\u2080 r : SA \u2192 \u211d)\n    (\u03b8 : Idx \u2192 \u211d)\n    (i : Idx)\n    (\u03a0_i' : Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (h\u03a0_i' :\n      HasDerivAt\n        (fun x : \u211d => \u03a0 (Function.update \u03b8 i x))\n        \u03a0_i'\n        (\u03b8 i))\n    (hInv :\n      \u2200 \u03b8\u2080 : Idx \u2192 \u211d, Matrix.det (1 - \u03a0 \u03b8\u2080) \u2260 0) :\n    HasDerivAt\n      (fun x : \u211d =>\n        dotProduct\n          (Matrix.vecMul p\u2080 ((1 - \u03a0 (Function.update \u03b8 i x))\u207b\u00b9))\n          r)\n      (dotProduct\n        (Matrix.vecMul p\u2080 ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9))\n        (Matrix.mulVec \u03a0_i'\n          (Matrix.mulVec ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9) r)))\n      (\u03b8 i) := by\n  sorry\n```\n\nThis version directly states:\n\n```text\nd/dx at x = \u03b8\u1d62 of\n    p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8 with \u03b8\u1d62 := x))\u207b\u00b9 \u00b7 r\n\nequals\n\n    p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 \u00b7 \u03a0_i' \u00b7 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r.\n```\n\nThat is the actual mathematical content.\n\nThe key differences from your original statement are:\n\n1. **`q` is not assumed to satisfy the Bellman equation.**\n   It is defined as\n\n   ```lean\n   Matrix.mulVec ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9) r\n   ```\n\n2. **`d` is not assumed to satisfy the visitation equation.**\n   It is defined as\n\n   ```lean\n   Matrix.vecMul p\u2080 ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8)\u207b\u00b9)\n   ```\n\n3. **`J` is not implicit.**\n   It is explicitly the scalar function\n\n   ```lean\n   fun \u03b8\u2080 =>\n     dotProduct\n       (Matrix.vecMul p\u2080 ((1 - \u03a0 \u03b8\u2080)\u207b\u00b9))\n       r\n   ```\n\n4. **`\u03a0_i'` is tied to an actual derivative hypothesis.**\n   The assumption\n\n   ```lean\n   HasDerivAt\n     (fun x : \u211d => \u03a0 (Function.update \u03b8 i x))\n     \u03a0_i'\n     (\u03b8 i)\n   ```\n\n   says exactly that `\u03a0_i'` is the derivative of `\u03a0` when only coordinate `i` varies. In Mathlib, `HasDerivAt f f' x` is the predicate saying that `f` has derivative `f'` at `x`. ([Lean Community][1])\n\n5. **The inverse is explicitly present and guarded by an invertibility hypothesis.**\n   Mathlib\u2019s matrix inverse notation `A\u207b\u00b9` is total and is defined even when no inverse exists, becoming zero in the singular case; therefore a theorem using `A\u207b\u00b9` as the mathematical inverse must include a nonsingularity assumption such as `det A \u2260 0`. ([Lean Community][2])\n\nYour earlier theorem is still useful, but it should be demoted to a lemma, for example:\n\n```lean\ntheorem policyGradient_algebra_core\n    {SA : Type*} [Fintype SA] [DecidableEq SA]\n    (\u03a0 \u03a0' : Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (d d' q r : SA \u2192 \u211d)\n    (hq_fix : r = Matrix.mulVec (1 - \u03a0) q)\n    (hd_diff : Matrix.vecMul d' (1 - \u03a0) = Matrix.vecMul d \u03a0') :\n    dotProduct d' r = dotProduct d (Matrix.mulVec \u03a0' q) := by\n  sorry\n```\n\nThen the real theorem should prove the hypotheses of this algebraic core from the inverse definitions:\n\n```text\nq(\u03b8) = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r\nd(\u03b8) = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9\nJ(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r\n\u03a0_i' = \u2202\u1d62\u03a0(\u03b8)\n```\n\nand conclude:\n\n```text\n\u2202\u1d62J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) (\u2202\u1d62\u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8).\n```\n\nSo yes: for the self-contained theorem, the inverse is not optional. It is the central object.\n\n[1]: https://leanprover-community.github.io/mathlib4_docs/Mathlib/Analysis/Calculus/Deriv/Basic.html \"Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Basic\"\n[2]: https://leanprover-community.github.io/mathlib4_docs/Mathlib/LinearAlgebra/Matrix/NonsingularInverse.html \"Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse\""

succeeded in 0ms:\n# Walkthrough\n\nThis review used only the provided context surface: the plan handoff, natural-language statement and proof, enrichment notes, the current `candidate.lean`, the compile result, and the source PDF excerpted from Andy Jones's \"The Correct Proof\".\n\n## What theorem the file is trying to prove\n\nThe theorem statement in `candidate.lean` matches the matrix-coordinate form fixed in the plan handoff:\n\n- `q = (I - \u03a0(theta))\u207b\u00b9 r`\n- `d = p0 (I - \u03a0(theta))\u207b\u00b9`\n- `J(theta0) = dotProduct (p0 (I - \u03a0(theta0))\u207b\u00b9) r`\n- target derivative: `\u2202/\u2202theta_i J = d \u00b7 (Pi_i' q)`\n\nThis is the same route described in the natural-language proof and in the source PDF section \"The Correct Proof\", where the proof proceeds by differentiating the fixed-point identities\n\n- `p0 = d (I - \u03a0)`\n- `r = (I - \u03a0) q`.\n\n## How the Lean proof body maps to the paper proof\n\n### 1. Reduce to the one-variable slice\n\nAfter `dsimp` on line 36, the attempt introduces the slice variable `x` and defines:\n\n- lines 37-38: `A x = 1 - Pi (update theta i x)`\n- lines 39-40: `qS x = (A x)\u207b\u00b9 r`\n- lines 41-42: `dS x = p0 (A x)\u207b\u00b9`\n\nThis is exactly plan step 1. It rewrites the multivariate coordinate derivative into the single-variable curve `x \u21a6 update theta i x`.\n\n### 2. Turn determinant nonvanishing into inverse-cancellation data\n\nLines 43-45 prove `hA_unit`, namely that `det (A x)` is a unit for every `x`. This comes directly from the theorem hypothesis\n\n- `hInv : det (1 - Pi theta0) \u2260 0`.\n\nThat witness is then fed to the nonsingular-inverse lemmas from `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse`.\n\n### 3. Recover the fixed-point identity for `q`\n\nLines 46-57 prove\n\n- `Matrix.mulVec (A x) (qS x) = r`.\n\nThis is the Lean form of `r = (I - \u03a0) q`. The calculation is:\n\n1. expand `qS x` as `(A x)\u207b\u00b9 r`;\n2. reassociate matrix-vector multiplication with `Matrix.mulVec_mulVec`;\n3. replace `A x * (A x)\u207b\u00b9` by `1` using the nonsingular-inverse lemma;\n4. simplify `1 * r = r`.\n\nThis matches plan step 2 and the natural-language proof's second fixed-point identity.\n\n### 4. Recover the fixed-point identity for `d`\n\nLines 58-69 prove\n\n- `Matrix.vecMul (dS x) (A x) = p0`.\n\nThis is the Lean form of `p0 = d (I - \u03a0)`. The proof is the row-vector analogue of the previous calculation:\n\n1. expand `dS x` as `p0 (A x)\u207b\u00b9`;\n2. reassociate with `Matrix.vecMul_vecMul`;\n3. replace `(A x)\u207b\u00b9 * A x` by `1`;\n4. simplify `p0 * 1 = p0`.\n\nThis matches the other fixed-point identity from the source proof.\n\n### 5. Compute the entrywise derivative of `A`\n\nLines 70-77 prove that every entry of `A` has derivative `-Pi_i' a b` at `x = theta i`.\n\nThis is the coordinatewise version of differentiating\n\n- `A(x) = I - \u03a0(update theta i x)`.\n\nSo far the attempt stays faithful to the locked theorem surface: it uses only the entrywise derivative hypothesis `hPi_i'`, not any stronger matrix-valued differentiability theorem.\n\n## Where the attempt stops\n\nThe proof ends with a comment block at lines 78-91 and a final `sorry` on line 92.\n\nThat means the attempt never carries out the remaining proof steps from the handoff:\n\n1. differentiate the fixed-point identities for `dS` and `qS`;\n2. extract the derivative identities\n   - `dS'(theta i) * A(theta i) = d * Pi_i'`\n   - `A(theta i) * qS'(theta i) = Pi_i' * q`;\n3. differentiate `J`;\n4. substitute `r = A(theta i) q` and conclude `dotProduct d (Matrix.mulVec Pi_i' q)`.\n\n## Repair-facing conclusion\n\nThe current attempt gets the algebraic setup right. It successfully formalizes the slice definitions and both fixed-point identities, and it computes the derivative of `A` entrywise. The missing part is the analytic bridge from those entrywise facts to derivatives of the inverse-dependent functions `qS` and `dS`.\n\nThe provided context explicitly flags that gap: the plan handoff and `relevant_lean_objects.md` both say that no matrix norm scope has been fixed on the locked surface, so inverse differentiability for matrix-valued functions is not yet available as a ready-to-use tool from the given imports alone. The review therefore should treat the attempt as structurally aligned with the intended proof, but still incomplete.\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' policyGradient_matrix_coordinate\n    {Idx SA : Type*}\n    [DecidableEq Idx]\n    [Fintype SA]\n    [DecidableEq SA]\n    (Pi : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (p0 r : SA \u2192 \u211d)\n    (theta : Idx \u2192 \u211d)\n    (i : Idx)\n    (Pi_i' : Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (hInv : \u2200 theta0 : Idx \u2192 \u211d, Matrix.det ((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta0) \u2260 0)\n    (hPi_i' :\n      \u2200 a b : SA,\n        HasDerivAt\n          (fun x : \u211d => Pi (Function.update theta i x) a b)\n          (Pi_i' a b)\n          (theta i)) :\n    let q : SA \u2192 \u211d :=\n      Matrix.mulVec (((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta)\u207b\u00b9) r\n    let d : SA \u2192 \u211d :=\n      Matrix.vecMul p0 (((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta)\u207b\u00b9)\n The usable statement is the finite-state, finite-action matrix form of the policy gradient theorem:\n\n- `\u03a0(\u03b8)[(s,a),(s',a')] = P(s' | s, a) * \u03c0_\u03b8(a' | s')`\n- `q(\u03b8) = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r`\n- `d(\u03b8) = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9`\n- `J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r = p\u2080 \u00b7 q(\u03b8)`\n- for each parameter coordinate `\u03b8_i`, `\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`\n\n`natural_language_statement.md` restates the theorem in prose, and `natural_language_proof.md` contains an honest natural-language proof.\n\n## Provenance\n\n- `artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/00_input/source.pdf` contains Andy L. Jones, \"A Clearer Proof of the Policy Gradient Theorem\" at the front of the PDF. That later material was not needed once the Jones proof was identified.\n\n## Key Lean Reuse\n\nThe most important reusable Lean objects are summarized in `relevant_lean_objects.md`. The main ones are:\n\n- `Matrix.mulVec`, `Matrix.vecMul`, and `dotProduct` / `\u2b1d\u1d65` for the row-vector / matrix / column-vector algebra.\n- `Matrix.dotProduct_mulVec`, `Matrix.vecMul_sub`, `Matrix.mulVec_sub`, and the bundled linear maps `Matrix.mulVecLin` and `Matrix.vecMulLinear`.\n- Matrix inverse and cancellation lemmas from `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse`, such as `Matrix.mul_inv_of_invertible`, `Matrix.inv_mul_of_invertible`, `Matrix.mul_inv_cancel_left_of_invertible`, and `Matrix.inv_mul_cancel_right_of_invertible`.\n- `HasFDerivAt.mul` and the inverse-derivative lemmas around `Ring.inverse` if later stages formalize differentiability of `(I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9` directly rather than taking it as a hypothesis.\n\n## Important Caveats\n\n- The reviewer file currently says `decision: reject`. Later stages should build the reinforcement-learning objects locally and reuse generic matrix and calculus infrastructure.\n- If later stages formalize matrix-valued calculus, they will need to choose an explicit matrix norm scope, since mathlib does not declare one default normed-ring structure for matrices.\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/01_enrichment/review.md\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Enrichment Approval\n\ndecision: approve\n\nChange `decision: pending` to `approve` when you want Terry to continue.\nLeave the `Notes:` section blank unless you want Terry to receive actual reviewer guidance.\n\nNotes:\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/01_enrichment/natural_language_proof.md\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Natural-Language Proof\n\nUnder the stated assumptions, the value vector and visitation vector satisfy the fixed-point identities\n\n- `d(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u03a0(\u03b8) + p\u2080`\n- `q(\u03b8) = \u03a0(\u03b8) q(\u03b8) + r`\n\nor equivalently\n\n- `p\u2080 = d(\u03b8) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))`\n- `r = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nDifferentiate the first identity with respect to a parameter coordinate `\u03b8_i`. Since `p\u2080` does not depend on `\u03b8`, the derivative is\n\n`0 = (\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) - d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8))`.\n\nRearranging gives\n\n`(\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) = d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8))`.\n\nNow differentiate the second identity. Since `r` is also parameter-independent, we get\n\n`0 = -(\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8) + (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) (\u2202_i q(\u03b8))`,\n\nso\n\n`(I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) (\u2202_i q(\u03b8)) = (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nFinally, differentiate the total return. Using `J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r`,\n\n`\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = (\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) \u00b7 r`.\n\nSubstitute the fixed-point formula `r = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`:\n\n`\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = (\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nNow apply the differentiated first fixed-point identity:\n\n`\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nThis is exactly the policy gradient formula.\n\nThe same conclusion also follows symmetrically from `J(\u03b8) = p\u2080 \u00b7 q(\u03b8)` together with the differentiated equation for `q(\u03b8)`. The proof therefore uses only the fixed-point identities for `d` and `q`, plus ordinary product-rule differentiation.\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/01_enrichment/natural_language_statement.md\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Natural-Language Statement\n\nFix finite sets of states and actions, a parameter-independent transition kernel `P(s' | s, a)`, a parameterized policy `\u03c0_\u03b8(a | s)` that is continuously differentiable in the parameter `\u03b8`, a reward vector `r`, and an initial state-action distribution `p\u2080`.\n\nFrom `P` and `\u03c0_\u03b8`, form the state-action transition matrix `\u03a0(\u03b8)` whose entry from `(s,a)` to `(s',a')` is the probability of first moving to state `s'` under the environment and then choosing action `a'` under the policy. Assume the resulting dynamics are discounted or otherwise transient enough that `I - \u03a0(\u03b8)` is invertible for every parameter under consideration.\n\nDefine the state-action value vector `q(\u03b8)` to be the expected total future reward from each state-action pair, define the visitation-frequency vector `d(\u03b8)` to be the expected number of visits to each state-action pair, and define the total return `J(\u03b8)` from the initial distribution. In matrix form these are `q(\u03b8) = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r`, `d(\u03b8) = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9`, and `J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r = p\u2080 \u00b7 q(\u03b8)`.\n\nThen the derivative of the total return with respect to any parameter coordinate is obtained by placing the derivative of the transition matrix between the visitation frequencies and the value vector. In other words, for every coordinate `\u03b8_i`, the partial derivative of `J` with respect to `\u03b8_i` is `d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/01_enrichment/proof_status.json\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/01_enrichment/relevant_lean_objects.md\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\n succeeded in 0ms:\n{\n  \"obtained\": true,\n  \"source\": \"artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/01_enrichment/review.md, corroborated by artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/00_input/source.pdf (Andy L. Jones, \\\"A Clearer Proof of the Policy Gradient Theorem\\\", first embedded article in the PDF).\",\n  \"notes\": \"The reviewer notes provide the explicit finite-state assumptions A1-A3 and a rigorous fixed-point proof. The reviewer file still records decision: reject, so downstream execution should wait for human approval.\"\n}\n\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Relevant Lean Objects\n\n## Summary\n\nI did not locate an existing reinforcement-learning theorem or MDP API in mathlib for the policy gradient theorem. The promising reuse path is to model the theorem with generic finite-dimensional matrix and calculus infrastructure.\n\n## Core Matrix and Vector Operations\n\n- Module: `Mathlib.Data.Matrix.Mul`\n- Key objects:\n  - `Matrix.mulVec`\n  - `Matrix.vecMul`\n  - `dotProduct` with notation `\u2b1d\u1d65`\n  - `Matrix.dotProduct_mulVec`\n  - `Matrix.vecMul_sub`\n  - `Matrix.mulVec_sub`\n  - `Matrix.vecMul_vecMul`\n  - `Matrix.mulVec_mulVec`\n  - `Matrix.vecMulVec`\n- Why they fit:\n  - They match the reviewer/source presentation almost directly: `d` and `p\u2080` are row vectors, `q` and `r` are column vectors, `\u03a0` is a square matrix, and `J` is a dot product.\n  - `Matrix.dotProduct_mulVec` is especially useful for switching between `J = d \u2b1d\u1d65 r` and `J = (d \u1d65* \u03a0) \u2b1d\u1d65 q`.\n- Notes:\n  - A clean encoding is `\u03a0 : Matrix SA SA \u211d` and `d p\u2080 q r : SA \u2192 \u211d` for a finite type `SA` of state-action pairs.\n  - `Matrix.vecMul_vecMul` and `Matrix.mulVec_mulVec` are the basic associativity lemmas for repeated vector/matrix actions.\n  - `Matrix.vecMulVec` is the rank-one matrix constructor if a proof chooses a matrix-only encoding.\n\n## Bundled Linear Maps\n\n- Module: `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.ToLin`\n- Key objects:\n  - `Matrix.mulVecLin`\n  - `Matrix.vecMulLinear`\n  - `dotProductBilin`\n  - `Matrix.mulVecLin_mul`\n- Why they fit:\n  - If later stages want a Fr\u00e9chet-derivative proof, these packaged linear maps are a more natural target than rewriting everything by hand on functions.\n  - `Matrix.mulVecLin_mul` packages composition of matrix actions as composition of linear maps.\n- Notes:\n  - These objects are also useful for translating between matrix inversion and inversion of continuous linear maps if that route is taken.\n\n## Matrix Inverses and Cancellation\n\n- Module: `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse`\n- Key objects:\n  - `Matrix.mul_inv_of_invertible`\n  - `Matrix.inv_mul_of_invertible`\n  - `Matrix.mul_inv_cancel_left_of_invertible`\n  - `Matrix.inv_mul_cancel_right_of_invertible`\n  - `Matrix.inv_mul_eq_iff_eq_mul_of_invertible`\n  - `Matrix.mul_inv_eq_iff_eq_mul_of_invertible`\n- Why they fit:\n  - The theorem surface defines `q` and `d` through `(I - \u03a0)\u207b\u00b9`.\n  - These lemmas are the obvious tools for deriving or simplifying the fixed-point identities from the inverse formulas.\n- Notes:\n  - The exact later hypothesis may be either an `Invertible (1 - \u03a0)` instance or an `IsUnit (1 - \u03a0)` assumption converted into such an instance.\n\n## Calculus on Products and Inverses\n\n- Modules:\n  - `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.FDeriv.Mul`\n  - `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.ContDiff.Operations`\n- Key objects:\n  - `HasFDerivAt.mul`\n  - `hasFDerivAt_ringInverse`\n  - `contDiffAt_ringInverse`\n- Why they fit:\n  - The reviewer notes justify that `q(\u03b8)` and `d(\u03b8)` are `C^1` because matrix inversion is smooth on invertible matrices.\n  - If later stages want to prove that smoothness in Lean instead of postulating it, these are the relevant reuse points.\n- Important gap:\n  - `hasFDerivAt_ringInverse` is stated for normed rings with summable geometric series and is phrased using `Ring.inverse`.\n  - Working with matrices requires choosing a concrete normed-ring structure first; mathlib deliberately does not install one by default for matrices.\n\n## Matrix Norms for Calculus\n\n- Module: `Mathlib.Analysis.Matrix.Normed`\n- Relevant scopes:\n  - `open scoped Matrix.Norms.Elementwise`\n  - `open scoped Matrix.Norms.Frobenius`\n  - `open scoped Matrix.Norms.Operator`\n- Why they fit:\n  - Any calculus proof over matrix-valued functions needs a normed additive group / normed ring instance on matrices.\n- Important gap:\n  - This is likely the first technical decision later stages must make.\n  - The `Matrix.Norms.Operator` scope is a plausible fit if one wants a normed-ring structure compatible with the usual operator viewpoint.\n\n## Geometric-Series / Neumann-Series Infrastructure\n\n- Modules:\n  - `Mathlib.Analysis.Normed.Ring.Units`\n  - `Mathlib.Topology.Algebra.InfiniteSum.Ring`\n- Key objects:\n  - `NormedRing.inverse_one_sub`\n  - `NormedRing.inverse_add`\n  - generic `tsum` / geometric-series lemmas in the infinite-sum ring library\n- Why they fit:\n  - These are relevant if later stages want to formalize the infinite-series definitions of `q` and `d`, or prove invertibility from a small-norm assumption.\n- Important gap:\n  - The reviewer notes use the stronger spectral-radius or invertibility assumption. I did not find a ready-made mathlib lemma during this scan that directly turns `spectralRadius \u03a0 < 1` into invertibility of `1 - \u03a0` in the matrix setting.\n  - Because of that, the safest first formalization pass is probably to assume `I - \u03a0(\u03b8)` is invertible, rather than trying to discharge A1 from spectral-radius facts.\n\n## Spectrum-Related Infrastructure\n\n- Module: `Mathlib.Analysis.Matrix.Spectrum`\n- Key objects:\n  - `Matrix.spectrum_toEuclideanLin`\n- Why it might fit:\n  - This is the obvious entry point if later stages insist on formalizing A1 in spectral language.\n- Important gap:\n  - The scan surfaced spectrum support, but not a ready-to-use bridge theorem from spectral-radius bounds to the exact inverse/summability facts needed here.\n\n## Recommended Formalization Shape\n\n- Use a finite type `SA` for state-action pairs.\n- Keep the theorem generic over `SA` and real-valued matrices/vectors.\n- Reuse `Matrix.vecMul`, `Matrix.mulVec`, `dotProduct`, and inverse lemmas to prove the fixed-point identities.\n- Prefer the fixed-point differentiation proof over a direct derivative-of-the-inverse proof unless smoothness of inversion is needed elsewhere; it matches the source more closely and avoids unnecessary analytic overhead.\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/00_input/provenance.json\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\n succeeded in 0ms:\n{\n  \"source\": {\n    \"kind\": \"pdf\",\n    \"path\": \"examples/inputs/policy-gradient-theorem.pdf\"\n  },\n  \"source_snapshot_path\": \"00_input/source.pdf\"\n}\n\nexec\n/bin/zsh -lc \"pdftotext -layout artifacts/runs/PG-theorem/00_input/source.pdf - | sed -n '1,260p'\" in /var/folders/fy/n_7qxz0s3h32g3p8_lnj6c6c0000gn/T/terry_codex_stage_o4j5wahr\n succeeded in 127ms:\nandy jones\n\n\n\nA Clearer Proof of the Policy Gradient\nTheorem\nThe policy gradient theorem is a foundational result in reinforcement learning. It turns the derivative\nof the total reward (hard!) into the derivative of the policy (easy!). Unfortunately, the usual proof uses\nmore algebraic manipulation than you'd like in a foundational result.\n\nInstead, this proof starts with an unusual view of reinforcement learning problems, a view from\nwhich you only need a small amount of maths to get to the theorem.\n\nThe View\nA common way to think about reinforcement learning is that each timestep you find yourself sitting in\na state, visualized as a node in a graph. You choose an action - an edge - out of the current state,\nand the environment takes you from that edge into a new node for the next timestep.\n\n                                                                s5\n                                                           a1\n                                          s3    a1    s4\n                                                           a2\n\n                                                                s6\n\n\n\nThis two-stage transition - node to edge and edge to node - is tricky to work with. It'd be much better\nif there were only one stage, but you can't ignore the edges entirely because there's valuable\ninformation - like rewards - that are defined by which action you take.\n\nWhat you can do instead is replace each edge with it's own node, representing a state-action pair:\n\n\n                                                           p5\n                                               p4\n                                                           p6\n\n\n\n\nNow each timestep has one stage: you're in a node, corresponding to a state-action, and between\nyou and the environment you - somehow - choose a new node for the next timestep. This muddies\nthe boundary between agent's behaviour and environment's behaviour, but for our purposes of\nproving the policy gradient theorem that's not a problem.\n\nThe Notation\n\fIn this way of thinking, you can stack the probability of finding yourself in each state-action to get a\nposition vector p. This means that pi is the probability of being in state-action i.\n\nThen to go from one position vector to the next, you simply multiply your current position vector by\nthe transition matrix:\n\n                                                 p\u2032 = p\u03a0\nIn this matrix, element \u03a0ij gives the probability of going from state-action i to state-action j. \u03a0\nmerges the behaviour of the environment with the behaviour of the agent, but again that's fine\nbecause the policy gradient theorem doesn't distinguish one from the other.\n\nSince transitioning is now simple as multiplication, if you start in a position distribution p0 then your\nposition after k steps has a nice form:\n\n                                               p(k) = p0\u03a0k\nMore, if r is a column vector of rewards for each state-action then the reward earned in the kth step\nhas a similarly nice form:\n\n                                              r(k) = p(k) \u22c5 r\nWith this notation in hand, the total reward earned by a policy can be written as\n\n                                       J = \u2211 r(k) = \u2211 p0\u03a0kr\n\nThis formula for the total reward can be parsed two ways. The first way is in terms of the state-\naction frequency d:\n\n                                  d = \u2211 p0\u03a0k          \u27f9          J =d\u22c5r\n\nThis says the total reward is how often you end up in each state-action, multiplied by the reward you\nget from being there.\n\nThe second way to parse the total reward is in terms of the state-action value q:\n\n                                  q = \u2211 \u03a0kr          \u27f9          J = p0 \u22c5 q\n\nThis says the total reward is simply the value of the initial state.\n\nTo recap,\n\n      p0 is a row vector giving the initial state-action distribution.\n      r is a column vector of the rewards for each state-action.\n      \u03a0 is the transition matrix, which gives the probability of going from one state-action to another\n      under the policy.\n      d is a row vector of the frequencies of visiting each state-action under the policy.\n      q is a column vector giving the values - the total future reward - of each state-action under the\n\f     policy.\n     J is the total reward earned by the policy.\n\nThe Simple Proof\nThe simple way to finish the proof is to act like all the matrices are actually real numbers. Then you\ncan use the geometric formula to write \u2211 \u03a0k = I\u2212\u03a0     I\n                                                          , so that\n\n                                  p0                     I               r\n                        d=                    J = p0         r    q=\n                                 I\u2212\u03a0                   I \u2212 \u03a0            I\u2212\u03a0\n                I\nThis matrix I\u2212\u03a0     has a simple interpretation: element ij is the number of times you'll visit state-\naction j if you start in state-action i.\n\nOnce you've got these three definitions, you can use high-school calculus to work out that the\ngradient of the total reward is\n                                                 \u2032\n                                                          \u03a0\u2032\n                                      p0(     ) r = p0\n                                 \u2032=        I\n                             J                                  r = d\u03a0\u2032q\n                                          I\u2212\u03a0          (I \u2212 \u03a0)2\n\nThat's the policy gradient theorem: J \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q. The left-hand side is hard, the right-hand side is\neasy. QED.\n\nKinda.\n\nThis approach 'works' in that it gives the right answer using rules you're familiar with, but it involves\nmore than one illegal operation. For one, I \u2212 \u03a0 can't be inverted. To do things properly requires a\nbit more indirection.\n\nThe Correct Proof\nAlthough q and d are easiest to intuit as sums, multiplying their definitions through by \u03a0 gives a\nneat defining identity for each one:\n\n                                       d = d\u03a0 + p0       q = \u03a0q + r\nor - gathering the terms -\n\n                                      p0 = d(I \u2212 \u03a0)      r = (I \u2212 \u03a0)q\nThey can also be turned into neat identities for the gradient:\n\n                                 d\u2032(I \u2212 \u03a0) = d\u03a0\u2032         (I \u2212 \u03a0)q \u2032 = \u03a0\u2032q\nGiven these identities, the policy gradient theorem falls out in two symmetric ways. The gradient of\nthe total reward is\n\f             J \u2032 = d\u2032 \u22c5 r = d\u2032(I \u2212 \u03a0)q = d\u03a0\u2032q              J \u2032 = p0 \u22c5 q \u2032 = d(I \u2212 \u03a0)q \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q\nOnce more, J \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q. QED.\n\nNotes\nAn especially nice thing about this notation is that J \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q has an obvious interpretation. J \u2032 will\nbe positive when \u03a0\u2032 increases the likelihood of transitions that go from oft-visited state-actions to\nhigh-value state-actions. Just the behaviour you want!\n\nYou can find the usual proof of the policy-gradient theorem on \u00a713.2/p269 of Sutton & Barto. The\nusual proof is more compact, but requires an infinite telescope of the gradient that confuses things.\nYou can find a version with more commentary here, or an alternative approach based on\nexpectations here.\n\nThanks to Adam Gleave and Jacob Hilton for comments and suggestions, and to Jerry Lin for\ncorrections.\n\nCitation\n\n @article{jones2020CPPGT,\n   title   = \"A Clearer Proof of the Policy Gradient Theorem\",\n   author = \"Andy L. Jones\",\n   journal = \"andyljones.com\",\n   year    = \"2020\",\n   url     = \"https://andyljones.com/posts/policy-gradient.html\"\n }\n\n\n2020/05/25\n                                      icons by dave gandy, theme by #6d2e98\n\f                                                                         Posts     Archive    Search       Tags   FAQ\n\n\n\n\nPolicy Gradient Algorithms\nDate: April 8, 2018 | Estimated Reading Time: 52 min | Author: Lilian Weng\n\n\n     \u25b6 Table of Contents\n\n\n\n[Updated on 2018-06-30: add two new policy gradient methods, SAC and D4PG.]\n[Updated on 2018-09-30: add a new policy gradient method, TD3.]\n[Updated on 2019-02-09: add SAC with automatically adjusted temperature].\n[Updated on 2019-06-26: Thanks to Chanseok, we have a version of this post in Korean].\n[Updated on 2019-09-12: add a new policy gradient method SVPG.]\n[Updated on 2019-12-22: add a new policy gradient method IMPALA.]\n[Updated on 2020-10-15: add a new policy gradient method PPG & some new discussion in PPO.]\n[Updated on 2021-09-19: Thanks to Wenhao & \u7231\u5403\u732b\u7684\u2ee5, we have this post in Chinese1 &\nChinese2].\n\n\nWhat is Policy Gradient\nPolicy gradient is an approach to solve reinforcement learning problems. If you haven\u2019t looked into\nthe field of reinforcement learning, please first read the section \u201cA (Long) Peek into Reinforcement\nLearning \u00bb Key Concepts\u201d for the problem definition and key concepts.\n\n\nNotations\nHere is a list of notations to help you read through equations in the post easily.\n\nSymbol             Meaning\n\ns\u2208S                States.\n\na\u2208A                Actions.\n\nr\u2208R                Rewards.\n\n                   State, action, and reward at time step t of one trajectory. I may occasionally use\nSt , At , Rt\n                   s t , a t , r t as well.\n\u03b3                  Discount factor; penalty to uncertainty of future rewards; 0 < \u03b3 \u2264 1.\n\nGt                 Return; or discounted future reward; G t = \u2211 \u221e    k\n                                                                k=0 \u03b3 R t+k+1\n                                                                              .\n\n                   Transition probability of getting to the next state s \u2032 from the current state s with\nP (s \u2032 , r|s, a)\n                   action a and reward r.\n\n\u03c0(a|s)             Stochastic policy (agent behavior strategy); \u03c0 \u03b8 (. ) is a policy parameterized by \u03b8.\n\f               Deterministic policy; we can also label this as \u03c0(s), but using a different letter gives\n\u00b5(s)           better distinction so that we can easily tell when the policy is stochastic or\n               deterministic without further explanation. Either \u03c0 or \u00b5 is what a reinforcement\n               learning algorithm aims to learn.\n\n               State-value function measures the expected return of state s; V w (. ) is a value\nV (s)\n               function parameterized by w.\n\nV \u03c0 (s)        The value of state s when we follow a policy \u03c0; V \u03c0 (s) = E a\u223c\u03c0 [G t |S t = s].\n\n               Action-value function is similar to V (s), but it assesses the expected return of a pair\nQ(s, a)\n               of state and action (s, a); Q w (. ) is a action value function parameterized by w.\n\n               Similar to V \u03c0 (. ), the value of (state, action) pair when we follow a policy \u03c0;\nQ \u03c0 (s, a)\n               Q \u03c0 (s, a) = E a\u223c\u03c0 [G t |S t = s, A t = a].\n               Advantage function, A(s, a) = Q(s, a) \u2212 V (s); it can be considered as another\nA(s, a)\n               version of Q-value with lower variance by taking the state-value off as the baseline.\n\n\n\nPolicy Gradient\nThe goal of reinforcement learning is to find an optimal behavior strategy for the agent to obtain\noptimal rewards. The policy gradient methods target at modeling and optimizing the policy\ndirectly. The policy is usually modeled with a parameterized function respect to \u03b8, \u03c0 \u03b8 (a|s). The\nvalue of the reward (objective) function depends on this policy and then various algorithms can be\napplied to optimize \u03b8 for the best reward.\n\nThe reward function is defined as:\n\n                      J(\u03b8) = \u2211 d \u03c0 (s)V \u03c0 (s) = \u2211 d \u03c0 (s) \u2211 \u03c0 \u03b8 (a|s)Q \u03c0 (s, a)\n                               s\u2208S                    s\u2208S        a\u2208A\n\nwhere d \u03c0 (s) is the stationary distribution of Markov chain for \u03c0 \u03b8 (on-policy state distribution under\n\u03c0). For simplicity, the parameter \u03b8 would be omitted for the policy \u03c0 \u03b8 when the policy is present in\n\ncodex\nI\u2019ve confirmed the context surface: the intended proof is Jones\u2019s differentiated fixed-point argument, and the prior compile failure is just the stale determinant import path. q))\n      (theta i) := by\n  sorry\n\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Natural-Language Proof\n\nUnder the stated assumptions, the value vector and visitation vector satisfy the fixed-point identities\n\n- `d(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u03a0(\u03b8) + p\u2080`\n- `q(\u03b8) = \u03a0(\u03b8) q(\u03b8) + r`\n\nor equivalently\n\n- `p\u2080 = d(\u03b8) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))`\n- `r = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nDifferentiate the first identity with respect to a parameter coordinate `\u03b8_i`. Since `p\u2080` does not depend on `\u03b8`, the derivative is\n\n`0 = (\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) - d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8))`.\n\nRearranging gives\n\n`(\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) = d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8))`.\n\nNow differentiate the second identity. Since `r` is also parameter-independent, we get\n\n`0 = -(\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8) + (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) (\u2202_i q(\u03b8))`,\n\nso\n\n`(I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) (\u2202_i q(\u03b8)) = (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nFinally, differentiate the total return. Using `J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r`,\n\n`\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = (\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) \u00b7 r`.\n\nSubstitute the fixed-point formula `r = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`:\n\n`\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = (\u2202_i d(\u03b8)) (I - \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nNow apply the differentiated first fixed-point identity:\n\n`\u2202_i J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\nThis is exactly the policy gradient formula.\n\nThe same conclusion also follows symmetrically from `J(\u03b8) = p\u2080 \u00b7 q(\u03b8)` together with the differentiated equation for `q(\u03b8)`. The proof therefore uses only the fixed-point identities for `d` and `q`, plus ordinary product-rule differentiation.\n\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Natural-Language Statement\n\nFix finite sets of states and actions, a parameter-independent transition kernel `P(s' | s, a)`, a parameterized policy `\u03c0_\u03b8(a | s)` that is continuously differentiable in the parameter `\u03b8`, a reward vector `r`, and an initial state-action distribution `p\u2080`.\n\nFrom `P` and `\u03c0_\u03b8`, form the state-action transition matrix `\u03a0(\u03b8)` whose entry from `(s,a)` to `(s',a')` is the probability of first moving to state `s'` under the environment and then choosing action `a'` under the policy. Assume the resulting dynamics are discounted or otherwise transient enough that `I - \u03a0(\u03b8)` is invertible for every parameter under consideration.\n\nDefine the state-action value vector `q(\u03b8)` to be the expected total future reward from each state-action pair, define the visitation-frequency vector `d(\u03b8)` to be the expected number of visits to each state-action pair, and define the total return `J(\u03b8)` from the initial distribution. In matrix form these are `q(\u03b8) = (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9 r`, `d(\u03b8) = p\u2080 (I - \u03a0(\u03b8))\u207b\u00b9`, and `J(\u03b8) = d(\u03b8) \u00b7 r = p\u2080 \u00b7 q(\u03b8)`.\n\nThen the derivative of the total return with respect to any parameter coordinate is obtained by placing the derivative of the transition matrix between the visitation frequencies and the value vector. In other words, for every coordinate `\u03b8_i`, the partial derivative of `J` with respect to `\u03b8_i` is `d(\u03b8) (\u2202_i \u03a0(\u03b8)) q(\u03b8)`.\n\n succeeded in 0ms:\n# Plan Handoff\n\n## Context Used\n\n- Read `01_enrichment/handoff.md`, `natural_language_statement.md`, `natural_language_proof.md`, `proof_status.json`, `relevant_lean_objects.md`, `00_input/provenance.json`, `00_input/source.pdf`, and the reviewer note at `02_plan/review.md`.\n- No `enrichment_review` pointer was provided in the stage inputs.\n- `source.pdf` confirms that the intended proof route is Andy Jones's fixed-point argument from the section headed \"The Correct Proof\".\n\n## Locked Imports\n\n```lean\nimport Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Basic\nimport Mathlib.Data.Matrix.Mul\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.Determinant\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse\n```\n\n## Locked Theorem Surface\n\n```lean\nimport Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Basic\nimport Mathlib.Data.Matrix.Mul\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.Determinant\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse\n\ntheorem policyGradient_matrix_coordinate\n    {Idx SA : Type*}\n    [DecidableEq Idx]\n    [Fintype SA]\n    [DecidableEq SA]\n    (Pi : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (p0 r : SA \u2192 \u211d)\n    (theta : Idx \u2192 \u211d)\n    (i : Idx)\n    (Pi_i' : Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (hInv : \u2200 theta0 : Idx \u2192 \u211d, Matrix.det ((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Basic\nimport Mathlib.Data.Matrix.Mul\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.Determinant\nimport Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.NonsingularInverse\n\ntheorem policyGradient_matrix_coordinate\n    {Idx SA : Type*}\n    [DecidableEq Idx]\n    [Fintype SA]\n    [DecidableEq SA]\n    (Pi : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (p0 r : SA \u2192 \u211d)\n    (theta : Idx \u2192 \u211d)\n    (i : Idx)\n    (Pi_i' : Matrix SA SA \u211d)\n    (hInv : \u2200 theta0 : Idx \u2192 \u211d, Matrix.det ((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta0) \u2260 0)\n    (hPi_i' :\n      \u2200 a b : SA,\n        HasDerivAt\n          (fun x : \u211d => Pi (Function.update theta i x) a b)\n          (Pi_i' a b)\n          (theta i)) :\n    let q : SA \u2192 \u211d :=\n      Matrix.mulVec (((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta)\u207b\u00b9) r\n    let d : SA \u2192 \u211d :=\n      Matrix.vecMul p0 (((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta)\u207b\u00b9)\n    let J : (Idx \u2192 \u211d) \u2192 \u211d :=\n      fun theta0 =>\n        dotProduct\n          (Matrix.vecMul p0 (((1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi theta0)\u207b\u00b9))\n          r\n    HasDerivAt\n      (fun x : \u211d => J (Function.update theta i x))\n      (dotProduct d (Matrix.mulVec Pi_i' q))\n      (theta i) := by\n  dsimp\n\n  -- Work on the one-coordinate slice `x \u21a6 update theta i x`.\n  let A : \u211d \u2192 Matrix SA SA \u211d := fun x =>\n    (1 : Matrix SA SA \u211d) - Pi (Function.update theta i x)\n  let qS : \u211d \u2192 SA \u2192 \u211d := fun x =>\n    Matrix.mulVec ((A x)\u207b\u00b9) r\n  let dS : \u211d \u2192 SA \u2192 \u211d := fun x =>\n    Matrix.vecMul p0 ((A x)\u207b\u00b9)\n\n  -- The determinant hypothesis guarantees that each `A x` is nonsingular.\n It turns the derivative\nof the total reward (hard!) into the derivative of the policy (easy!). Unfortunately, the usual proof uses\nmore algebraic manipulation than you'd like in a foundational result.\nInstead, this proof starts with an unusual view of reinforcement learning problems, a view from\nwhich you only need a small amount of maths to get to the theorem.\n\nThe View\nA common way to think about reinforcement learning is that each timestep you find yourself sitting in\na state, visualized as a node in a graph. You choose an action - an edge - out of the current state,\nand the environment takes you from that edge into a new node for the next timestep.\ns5\na1\n\ns3\n\na1\n\ns4\na2\n\ns6\n\nThis two-stage transition - node to edge and edge to node - is tricky to work with. It'd be much better\nif there were only one stage, but you can't ignore the edges entirely because there's valuable\ninformation - like rewards - that are defined by which action you take.\nWhat you can do instead is replace each edge with it's own node, representing a state-action pair:\n\np5\np4\np6\n\nNow each timestep has one stage: you're in a node, corresponding to a state-action, and between\nyou and the environment you - somehow - choose a new node for the next timestep. This muddies\nthe boundary between agent's behaviour and environment's behaviour, but for our purposes of\nproving the policy gradient theorem that's not a problem.\n\nThe Notation\n\n\fIn this way of thinking, you can stack the probability of finding yourself in each state-action to get a\nposition vector p. This means that pi is the probability of being in state-action i.\nThen to go from one position vector to the next, you simply multiply your current position vector by\nthe transition matrix:\n\np\u2032 = p\u03a0\nIn this matrix, element \u03a0ij gives the probability of going from state-action i to state-action j. \u03a0\nmerges the behaviour of the environment with the behaviour of the agent, but again that's fine\nbecause the policy gradient theorem doesn't distinguish one from the other.\nSince transitioning is now simple as multiplication, if you start in a position distribution p0 then your\nposition after k steps has a nice form:\n\np(k) = p0\u03a0k\nMore, if r is a column vector of rewards for each state-action then the reward earned in the kth step\nhas a similarly nice form:\n\nr(k) = p(k) \u22c5 r\nWith this notation in hand, the total reward earned by a policy can be written as\n\nJ = \u2211 r(k) = \u2211 p0\u03a0kr\nThis formula for the total reward can be parsed two ways. The first way is in terms of the stateaction frequency d:\n\nd = \u2211 p0\u03a0k\n\n\u27f9\n\nJ =d\u22c5r\n\nThis says the total reward is how often you end up in each state-action, multiplied by the reward you\nget from being there.\nThe second way to parse the total reward is in terms of the state-action value q:\n\nq = \u2211 \u03a0kr\n\n\u27f9\n\nJ = p0 \u22c5 q\n\nThis says the total reward is simply the value of the initial state.\nTo recap,\n\np0 is a row vector giving the initial state-action distribution.\nr is a column vector of the rewards for each state-action.\n\u03a0 is the transition matrix, which gives the probability of going from one state-action to another\n\nunder the policy.\nd is a row vector of the frequencies of visiting each state-action under the policy.\nq is a column vector giving the values - the total future reward - of each state-action under the\n\n\fpolicy.\nJ is the total reward earned by the policy.\n\nThe Simple Proof\nThe simple way to finish the proof is to act like all the matrices are actually real numbers. Then you\nI\ncan use the geometric formula to write \u2211 \u03a0k = I\u2212\u03a0\n, so that\n\nd=\n\np0\nI\u2212\u03a0\n\nJ = p0\n\nI\nr\n\u2212\n\u03a0\nI\n\nq=\n\nr\nI\u2212\u03a0\n\nI\nThis matrix I\u2212\u03a0\nhas a simple interpretation: element ij is the number of times you'll visit stateaction j if you start in state-action i.\n\nOnce you've got these three definitions, you can use high-school calculus to work out that the\ngradient of the total reward is\n\nJ\n\n\u2032=\n\n\u2032\n\nI\n\u03a0\u2032\np0(\n) r = p0\nr = d\u03a0\u2032q\n(I \u2212 \u03a0)2\nI\u2212\u03a0\n\nThat's the policy gradient theorem: J \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q. The left-hand side is hard, the right-hand side is\neasy. QED.\nKinda.\nThis approach 'works' in that it gives the right answer using rules you're familiar with, but it involves\nmore than one illegal operation. For one, I \u2212 \u03a0 can't be inverted. To do things properly requires a\nbit more indirection.\n\nThe Correct Proof\nAlthough q and d are easiest to intuit as sums, multiplying their definitions through by \u03a0 gives a\nneat defining identity for each one:\n\nd = d\u03a0 + p0\n\nq = \u03a0q + r\n\np0 = d(I \u2212 \u03a0)\n\nr = (I \u2212 \u03a0)q\n\nor - gathering the terms -\n\nThey can also be turned into neat identities for the gradient:\n\nd\u2032(I \u2212 \u03a0) = d\u03a0\u2032\n\n(I \u2212 \u03a0)q \u2032 = \u03a0\u2032q\n\nGiven these identities, the policy gradient theorem falls out in two symmetric ways. The gradient of\nthe total reward is\n\n\fJ \u2032 = d\u2032 \u22c5 r = d\u2032(I \u2212 \u03a0)q = d\u03a0\u2032q\n\nJ \u2032 = p0 \u22c5 q \u2032 = d(I \u2212 \u03a0)q \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q\n\nOnce more, J \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q. QED.\n\nNotes\nAn especially nice thing about this notation is that J \u2032 = d\u03a0\u2032q has an obvious interpretation. J \u2032 will\nbe positive when \u03a0\u2032 increases the likelihood of transitions that go from oft-visited state-actions to\nhigh-value state-actions. Just the behaviour you want!\nYou can find the usual proof of the policy-gradient theorem on \u00a713.2/p269 of Sutton & Barto. The\nusual proof is more compact, but requires an infinite telescope of the gradient that confuses things.\nYou can find a version with more commentary here, or an alternative approach based on\nexpectations here.\nThanks to Adam Gleave and Jacob Hilton for comments and suggestions, and to Jerry Lin for\ncorrections.\n\nCitation\n@article{jones2020CPPGT,\ntitle\n= \"A Clearer Proof of the Policy Gradient Theorem\",\nauthor = \"Andy L. Jones\",\njournal = \"andyljones.com\",\nyear\n= \"2020\",\nurl\n= \"https://andyljones.com/posts/policy-gradient.html\"\n}\n\n2020/05/25\nicons by dave gandy, theme by #6d2e98\n\n\f\nERROR: Reconnecting... 2/5\ncodex\nI\u2019ve confirmed the PDF matches the fixed-point proof route.    report Lean output (e.g., messages) as JSON (one per line)\n  -E, --error=kind       report Lean messages of kind as errors\n      --deps             just print dependencies of a Lean input\n ### Inverses of permutated matrices\n\nNote that the simp-normal form of `Matrix.reindex` is `Matrix.submatrix`, so we prove most of these\nresults about only the latter.\n-/\n\n\nsection Submatrix\n\nvariable [Fintype m]\nvariable [DecidableEq m]\n\n/-- `A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082` is invertible if `A` is -/\n@[implicit_reducible]\ndef submatrixEquivInvertible (A : Matrix m m \u03b1) (e\u2081 e\u2082 : n \u2243 m) [Invertible A] :\n    Invertible (A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082) :=\n  invertibleOfRightInverse _ ((\u215fA).submatrix e\u2082 e\u2081) <| by\n    rw [Matrix.submatrix_mul_equiv, mul_invOf_self, submatrix_one_equiv]\n\n/-- `A` is invertible if `A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082` is -/\n@[implicit_reducible]\ndef invertibleOfSubmatrixEquivInvertible (A : Matrix m m \u03b1) (e\u2081 e\u2082 : n \u2243 m)\n    [Invertible (A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082)] : Invertible A :=\n  invertibleOfRightInverse _ ((\u215f(A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082)).submatrix e\u2082.symm e\u2081.symm) <| by\n    have : A = (A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082).submatrix e\u2081.symm e\u2082.symm := by simp\n    conv in _ * _ =>\n      congr\n      rw [this]\n    rw [Matrix.submatrix_mul_equiv, mul_invOf_self, submatrix_one_equiv]\n\ntheorem invOf_submatrix_equiv_eq (A : Matrix m m \u03b1) (e\u2081 e\u2082 : n \u2243 m) [Invertible A]\n    [Invertible (A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082)] : \u215f(A.submatrix e\u2081 e\u2082) = (\u215fA).submatrix e\u2082 e\u2081 := by\n  rw [@Invertible.congr _ _ _ _ _ (submatrixEquivInvertible A e\u2081 e\u2082) rfl]\n\n succeeded in 0ms:\n/-\nCopyright (c) 2019 Anne Baanen. Mathlib.GroupTheory.GroupAction.Ring\n\n/-!\n# Cramer's rule and adjugate matrices\n\nThe adjugate matrix is the transpose of the cofactor matrix.\nIt is calculated with Cramer's rule, which we introduce first.\nThe vectors returned by Cramer's rule are given by the linear map `cramer`,\nwhich sends a matrix `A` and vector `b` to the vector consisting of the\ndeterminant of replacing the `i`th column of `A` with `b` at index `i`\n(written as `(A.updateCol i b).det`).\nUsing Cramer's rule, we can compute for each matrix `A` the matrix `adjugate A`.\nThe entries of the adjugate are the minors of `A`.\nInstead of defining a minor by deleting row `i` and column `j` of `A`, we\nreplace the `i`th row of `A` with the `j`th basis vector; the resulting matrix\nhas the same determinant but more importantly equals Cramer's rule applied\nto `A` and the `j`th basis vector, simplifying the subsequent proofs.\nWe prove the adjugate behaves like `det A \u2022 A\u207b\u00b9`.\n\n## Main definitions\n\n* `Matrix.cramer A b`: the vector output by Cramer's rule on `A` and `b`.\n* `Matrix.adjugate A`: the adjugate (or classical adjoint) of the matrix `A`.\n\n## References\n\n  * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cramer's_rule#Finding_inverse_matrix\n\n## Tags\n\ncramer, cramer's rule, adjugate\n-/\n\n@[expose] public section\n\n\nnamespace Matrix\n\nuniverse u v w\n\nvariable {m : Type u} {n : Type v} {\u03b1 : Type w}\nvariable [DecidableEq n] [Fintype n] [DecidableEq m] [Fintype m] [CommRing \u03b1]\n\nopen Matrix Polynomial Equiv Equiv.Perm Finset\n\nsection Cramer\n\n/-!\n  ### `cramer` section\n\n  Introduce the linear map `cramer` with values defined by `cramerMap`.\n  After defining `cramerMap` and showing it is linear,\n  we will restrict our proofs to using `cramer`.\n-/\n\n\nvariable (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) (b : n \u2192 \u03b1)\n\n/-- `cramerMap A b i` is the determinant of the matrix `A` with column `i` replaced with `b`,\n  and thus `cramerMap A b` is the vector output by Cramer's rule on `A` and `b`.\n\n  If `A * x = b` has a unique solution in `x`, `cramerMap A` sends the vector `b` to `A.det \u2022 x`.\n  Otherwise, the outcome of `cramerMap` is well-defined but not necessarily useful.\n-/\ndef cramerMap (i : n) : \u03b1 :=\n  (A.updateCol i b).det\n\ntheorem cramerMap_is_linear (i : n) : IsLinearMap \u03b1 fun b => cramerMap A b i :=\n  { map_add := det_updateCol_add _ _\n    map_smul := det_updateCol_smul _ _ }\n\ntheorem cramer_is_linear : IsLinearMap \u03b1 (cramerMap A) := by\n  constructor <;> intros <;> ext i\n  \u00b7 apply (cramerMap_is_linear A i).1\n  \u00b7 apply (cramerMap_is_linear A i).2\n\n/-- `cramer A b i` is the determinant of the matrix `A` with column `i` replaced with `b`,\n  and thus `cramer A b` is the vector output by Cramer's rule on `A` and `b`.\n\n  If `A * x = b` has a unique solution in `x`, `cramer A` sends the vector `b` to `A.det \u2022 x`.\n  Otherwise, the outcome of `cramer` is well-defined but not necessarily useful.\n-/\ndef cramer (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) : (n \u2192 \u03b1) \u2192\u2097[\u03b1] (n \u2192 \u03b1) :=\n  IsLinearMap.mk' (cramerMap A) (cramer_is_linear A)\n\ntheorem cramer_apply (i : n) : cramer A b i = (A.updateCol i b).det :=\n  rfl\n\ntheorem cramer_transpose_apply (i : n) : cramer A\u1d40 b i = (A.updateRow i b).det := by\n  rw [cramer_apply, updateCol_transpose, det_transpose]\n\ntheorem cramer_transpose_row_self (i : n) : A\u1d40.cramer (A i) = Pi.single i A.det := by\n  ext j\n  rw [cramer_apply, Pi.single_apply]\n  split_ifs with h\n  \u00b7 -- i = j: this entry should be `A.det`\n    subst h\n    simp only [updateCol_transpose, det_transpose, updateRow_eq_self]\n  \u00b7 -- i \u2260 j: this entry should be 0\n    rw [updateCol_transpose, det_transpose]\n    apply det_zero_of_row_eq h\n    rw [updateRow_self, updateRow_ne (Ne.symm h)]\n\ntheorem cramer_row_self (i : n) (h : \u2200 j, b j = A j i) : A.cramer b = Pi.single i A.det := by\n  rw [\u2190 transpose_transpose A, det_transpose]\n  convert cramer_transpose_row_self A\u1d40 i\n  exact funext h\n\n@[simp]\ntheorem cramer_one : cramer (1 : Matrix n n \u03b1) = 1 := by\n  ext i j\n  convert congr_fun (cramer_row_self (1 : Matrix n n \u03b1) (Pi.single i 1) i _) j\n  \u00b7 simp\n  \u00b7 intro j\n    rw [Matrix.one_eq_pi_single, Pi.single_comm]\n\ntheorem cramer_smul (r : \u03b1) (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) :\n    cramer (r \u2022 A) = r ^ (Fintype.card n - 1) \u2022 cramer A :=\n  LinearMap.ext fun _ => funext fun _ => det_updateCol_smul_left _ _ _ _\n\n@[simp]\ntheorem cramer_subsingleton_apply [Subsingleton n] (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) (b : n \u2192 \u03b1) (i : n) :\n    cramer A b i = b i := by rw [cramer_apply, det_eq_elem_of_subsingleton _ i, updateCol_self]\n\ntheorem cramer_zero [Nontrivial n] : cramer (0 : Matrix n n \u03b1) = 0 := by\n  ext i j\n  obtain \u27e8j', hj'\u27e9 : \u2203 j', j' \u2260 j := exists_ne j\n  apply det_eq_zero_of_column_eq_zero j'\n  simp [updateCol_ne hj']\n\n/-- Use linearity of `cramer` to take it out of a summation. -/\ntheorem sum_cramer {\u03b2} (s : Finset \u03b2) (f : \u03b2 \u2192 n \u2192 \u03b1) :\n    (\u2211 x \u2208 s, cramer A (f x)) = cramer A (\u2211 x \u2208 s, f x) :=\n  (map_sum (cramer A) ..).symm\n\n/-- Use linearity of `cramer` and vector evaluation to take `cramer A _ i` out of a summation. -/\ntheorem sum_cramer_apply {\u03b2} (s : Finset \u03b2) (f : n \u2192 \u03b2 \u2192 \u03b1) (i : n) :\n    (\u2211 x \u2208 s, cramer A (fun j => f j x) i) = cramer A (fun j : n => \u2211 x \u2208 s, f j x) i :=\n  calc\n    (\u2211 x \u2208 s, cramer A (fun j => f j x) i) = (\u2211 x \u2208 s, cramer A fun j => f j x) i :=\n      (Finset.sum_apply i s _).symm\n    _ = cramer A (fun j : n => \u2211 x \u2208 s, f j x) i := by\n      rw [sum_cramer, cramer_apply]\n      congr with j\n      apply Finset.sum_apply\n\ntheorem cramer_submatrix_equiv (A : Matrix m m \u03b1) (e : n \u2243 m) (b : n \u2192 \u03b1) :\n    cramer (A.submatrix e e) b = cramer A (b \u2218 e.symm) \u2218 e := by\n  ext i\n  simp_rw [Function.comp_apply, cramer_apply, updateCol_submatrix_equiv,\n    det_submatrix_equiv_self e, Function.comp_def]\n\ntheorem cramer_reindex (e : m \u2243 n) (A : Matrix m m \u03b1) (b : n \u2192 \u03b1) :\n    cramer (reindex e e A) b = cramer A (b \u2218 e) \u2218 e.symm :=\n  cramer_submatrix_equiv _ _ _\n\nend Cramer\n\nsection Adjugate\n\n/-!\n### `adjugate` section\n\nDefine the `adjugate` matrix and a few equations.\nThese will hold for any matrix over a commutative ring.\n-/\n\n\n/-- The adjugate matrix is the transpose of the cofactor matrix.\n\n  Typically, the cofactor matrix is defined by taking minors,\n  i.e. the determinant of the matrix with a row and column removed.\n  However, the proof of `mul_adjugate` becomes a lot easier if we use the\n  matrix replacing a column with a basis vector, since it allows us to use\n  facts about the `cramer` map.\n-/\ndef adjugate (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) : Matrix n n \u03b1 :=\n  of fun i => cramer A\u1d40 (Pi.single i 1)\n\ntheorem adjugate_def (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) : adjugate A = of fun i => cramer A\u1d40 (Pi.single i 1) :=\n  rfl\n\ntheorem adjugate_apply (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) (i j : n) :\n    adjugate A i j = (A.updateRow j (Pi.single i 1)).det := by\n  rw [adjugate_def, of_apply, cramer_apply, updateCol_transpose, det_transpose]\n\ntheorem adjugate_transpose (A : Matrix n n \u03b1) : (adjugate A)\u1d40 = adjugate A\u1d40 := by\n  ext i j\n  rw [transpose_apply, adjugate_apply, adjugate_apply, updateRow_transpose, det_transpose]\n  rw [det_apply', det_apply']\n  apply Finset.sum_congr rfl\n  intro \u03c3 _\n  congr 1\n  by_cases h : i = \u03c3 j\n  \u00b7 -- Everything except `(i, j)` (= `(\u03c3 j, j)`) is given by A, and the rest is a single `1`.\n    congr\n    ext j'\n    subst h\n    have : \u03c3 j' = \u03c3 j \u2194 j' = j := \u03c3.injective.eq_iff\n    rw [updateRow_apply, updateCol_apply]\n    simp_rw [this]\n    rw [\u2190 dite_eq_ite, \u2190 dite_eq_ite]\n    congr 1 with rfl\n
